summary: Task 3 wires ResetReason through every ChatViewModel callsite and reclassifies MAX_TOKENS as HEAVY without introducing any direct ErrorLog interpolation, hardcoded secret, or new permission surface. Sanitization remains owned by DefaultModelRegistry's Task 2 pipeline (success log: `reason=${reason.name}`; skip log: `skipped reason=... status=...`) — neither path ever interpolates the user-controlled system prompt or the merged config map, so the verbatim `effectiveSystemPrompt(merged)` value passed into `registry.resetConversation` flows straight into `Contents.of(...)` without touching errors.log. lifecycleMutex still serialises every reset on Dispatchers.Default and `model.configValues` is mutated on Main before the dispatcher hop, which provides the required happens-before for the Default-side read inside the mutex; `_chatModelId` is a StateFlow throughout. One MAJOR concern: in the Persistent bootstrap, `observeFirstReadyThenReset` and `observeFirstReadyThenResume` are launched as siblings in viewModelScope and both await `_uiState.first { Ready }`. Because `registry.resetConversation` performs `withContext(Dispatchers.Default)` before acquiring `lifecycleMutex`, the reset coroutine yields the Main dispatcher BEFORE the underlying `helper.resetConversation` actually runs — the resume coroutine then resumes on Main and calls `helper.runInference` synchronously against the still-dirty Conversation, defeating AC-1.2 for the DRAFT_COMMIT path the very task was built to fix. One MINOR concern: `observeFirstReadyThenReset` suspends indefinitely on a perma-Failed engine; cancellation is correct via `viewModelScope`, but no resource leak.

findings.title: Bootstrap reset races with auto-resume on DRAFT_COMMIT path — KV-cache from prior chat can taint the auto-resumed first answer
findings.description: Both `observeFirstReadyThenReset(resetReason)` and `observeFirstReadyThenResume(id)` are launched as independent children of viewModelScope and both await `_uiState.first { it is ChatUiState.Ready }`. viewModelScope is backed by Dispatchers.Main.immediate; on the same Ready emission both continuations become runnable on Main in launch order. The reset coroutine resumes first, computes effective system prompt, then enters `registry.resetConversation(...)` whose body is `withContext(Dispatchers.Default) { lifecycleMutex.withLock { helper.resetConversation(...) } }`. The `withContext(Dispatchers.Default)` boundary suspends the Main coroutine BEFORE the helper-level reset has executed, releasing Main back to the dispatcher loop. The resume coroutine (still on Main) then runs `resumePendingAssistantPersistent(...)` → `dispatchInferencePersistent(...)` → `helper.runInference(model, input, ...)` synchronously. `helper.runInference` is a non-suspending fun that engages the engine's Conversation immediately — that Conversation is the one the registry has not yet recreated. Result: the auto-resumed first answer to the unpaired USER row inherits KV-cache from the previously active chat, exactly the leak Task 3 was wired to prevent (Bug 1, AC-1.2 / DRAFT_COMMIT). The unit test `bootstrapPersistent_emitsDraftCommitReset_whenLastIsUnpairedUser` does not exercise the helper.runInference timing under the production dispatcher, so the race is invisible to the suite. Not a confidentiality breach against an external attacker, but content from one chat leaking into another chat's first answer is a privacy-relevant data-integrity violation in a local-first messaging product. CWE-362 (race condition) / CWE-200-adjacent (information leak across logical boundaries).
findings.impact: On Draft→Persistent handover the very first auto-resumed answer can be biased by the previous chat's accumulated KV state — partially nullifying AC-1.2 in the exact scenario its DRAFT_COMMIT tag was designed to flag. Same race exists for CHAT_SWITCH if the previous Persistent VM had already armed an auto-resume but did not run before the user navigated; the new VM's auto-resume can fire before the new VM's reset reaches the helper.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) gate `observeFirstReadyThenResume` on the reset coroutine completing — inline-await reset in the same coroutine before invoking resume (single coroutine: wait Ready → suspend on `registry.resetConversation` → resume pending USER); (b) make `helper.runInference` go through a registry method that holds `lifecycleMutex` for the duration of its setup, naturally serialising it behind the in-flight reset; or (c) add a Job handshake — store the reset Job from the launch and have the resume coroutine `resetJob.join()` after its `_uiState.first { Ready }`. Option (a) is the smallest change and matches the existing pattern of `observeFirstReadyThenReset`. Add a regression test that registers ordered helper calls and asserts `helper.resetConversation` runs strictly before `helper.runInference` on the DRAFT_COMMIT path.

findings.title: Cold-start reset coroutine is silently parked forever on a perma-Failed engine — no diagnostic trace
findings.description: If the engine never reaches Ready (Failed → user dismisses → VM stays alive), the reset coroutine remains suspended indefinitely. Cancellation is sound: viewModelScope.cancel() on VM clear collapses it cooperatively, so this is NOT a resource leak. However, unlike `observeFirstReadyThenResume` (which carries a parallel auto-resume UX hook), the reset has no user-visible side-effect on Failed and silently consumes the AC-1.1/AC-1.2 invariant for that VM lifetime. There is no `errorLog.w("inference-reset", "reset never armed: terminal Failed")` to make the lost reset visible in errors.log, even though every neighbouring path on this surface area (Task 1 whitelist already covers `inference-reset`) is wired to log.
findings.impact: Diagnostic gap only — no security boundary crossed. A QA engineer triaging a Honor-200 device-smoke `Failed` repro cannot distinguish 'reset skipped because not Ready' (logged by DefaultModelRegistry) from 'reset never reached the registry because engine perma-failed' (silent).
findings.recommendation: Optional follow-up: race the Ready predicate against a terminal Failed predicate using `_uiState.transformWhile`/`first { Ready || terminalFailed }` and emit a single `errorLog.w("inference-reset", "never armed: status=Failed reason=${reason.name}")` if Failed wins. Out-of-scope for Task 3 if the fix would expand surface area; track as Task 6 dependency since Task 6 already touches this file with `engineReady: StateFlow<Boolean>`.

findings.title: System-prompt content leak path verified clean
findings.description: Every new and amended `registry.resetConversation(model.name, systemPrompt = effective, reason = ...)` callsite passes `effectiveSystemPrompt(merged)` (user-controlled — sourced from PerModelSettings via SYSTEM_PROMPT_DEFAULT) only as the `systemPrompt` parameter. DefaultModelRegistry never interpolates that value into its log lines (`errorLog.i(LOG_TAG_RESET, "reason=${reason.name}")` and `errorLog.w(LOG_TAG_RESET, "skipped reason=... status=...")`); it is wrapped in `Contents.of(...)` and forwarded to `helper.resetConversation`. ChatViewModel itself adds NO new `errorLog.*` call in this commit. Result: no parallel formatter, no bypass of Task 1's `write(level, ...)` sanitize+truncate pipeline, no system-prompt content reaches errors.log. Recording this as info to confirm the prompt's check-2 explicitly.

findings.title: Mutex preservation and StateFlow visibility on the Light-overrides path verified
findings.description: `applyLightOverrides` mutates `model.configValues = merged` on Main, then suspends across `withContext(Dispatchers.Default)` inside `registry.resetConversation` before `helper.resetConversation` reads `entry.model.configValues` under `lifecycleMutex`. The dispatcher hop provides the happens-before edge needed for the new-config read on Default to observe the Main-side write. `_chatModelId` is `MutableStateFlow` throughout (already-safe publication semantics). No unsynchronised access introduced. lifecycleMutex still owns every reset path end-to-end. Confirms prompt's check-3.
